phenomenon.name: Hobgoblin

aliases.name: Hob
aliases.meaning: A familiar diminutive of 'Robert', used for household sprites

aliases.name: Robin Goodfellow
aliases.meaning: Most famous individual hobgoblin; identified with Puck

aliases.name: Puck
aliases.meaning: Mischievous sprite of English folklore identified by Shakespeare with the hobgoblin

phenomenon.subcategory: Household sprite / mischievous sprite of British folklore

description.summary: A small, hairy household sprite of English and broader British folklore, originally helpful or merely mischievous and only later conflated with malevolent goblins.
description.full: A hobgoblin is a small humanoid spirit of English folklore, traditionally distinguished from goblins by being friendly or merely mischievous rather than malevolent. The first element 'hob' (attested in English by around 1530) is a familiar diminutive of 'Robert' and was used for household sprites associated with the hearth — particularly the 'hob', the flat metal shelf or recess beside a fireplace. Hobgoblins are typically described as small, hairy, often dressed in rags, and attached to a particular farm or household, performing nightly chores in exchange for offerings of milk or bread. Like brownies, they may turn destructive (becoming a 'boggart' in some traditions) if mistreated or formally paid. The most famous hobgoblin is Robin Goodfellow, identified by Shakespeare with Puck in A Midsummer Night's Dream (c. 1595). After the Reformation and the gradual Christianisation of English folklore, the term 'hobgoblin' shifted in meaning, drifting toward the modern sense of a small malevolent goblin. Regional names for hobgoblin-like figures include Dobby in Lancashire and Yorkshire, the Scottish bauchan, and the Welsh bwbach.

etymology.origin: Middle English compound from 'hob' (a familiar diminutive of the personal name 'Robert', used for household sprites) and 'goblin' (a small malevolent or mischievous creature, ultimately from medieval Latin gobelinus).
etymology.meaning: Originally 'a hob's goblin' or 'goblin of the hob (hearth)', i.e. a household sprite. The modern sense has often shifted toward 'a small frightening goblin', inverting the original friendly connotation.
etymology.first_use: 'Hob' as a name for a household sprite is attested by c. 1530 in English.

related_phenomena.name: Brownie
related_phenomena.description: Helpful Scottish household sprite very close in role and behaviour

related_phenomena.name: Puck
related_phenomena.description: Mischievous English sprite identified by Shakespeare as a hobgoblin

related_phenomena.name: Boggart
related_phenomena.description: Malevolent or hostile counterpart to a household hobgoblin in northern English tradition

related_phenomena.name: Goblin
related_phenomena.description: Generic small malevolent humanoid sprite; modern usage has partly conflated the two

related_phenomena.name: Bauchan
related_phenomena.description: Scottish Highland equivalent of the hobgoblin

features.description: Diminutive humanoid shape, typically waist-high or smaller
Often described as hairy, sometimes nearly naked under tatters of clothing
Tatters, ill-fitting cast-offs, or simple peasant dress; gifts of new clothing are traditionally said to drive a hob away
Mischievous, grinning face; in the Robin Goodfellow tradition, sometimes shown with a broom or lantern

physical.morphology: Small hairy humanoid; physical descriptions vary widely between regions and between literary and oral traditions.

abilities.description: Cleans, threshes, dusts, churns butter, and tidies during the night, in exchange for milk or bread
Many hobgoblin tales, especially the Robin Goodfellow stories, depict the sprite changing form to play tricks
Pinches, hides objects, leads travellers astray, sours milk, and pulls the bedclothes off sleepers

abilities.ability: Departure if formally paid or clothed
abilities.description: Will leave a household forever if rewarded with new clothes or an explicit wage instead of customary offerings

behavior.diet: Traditional offerings of milk, cream, bread, or porridge left out at night.
patterns: Attaches itself to a single house or farm, sometimes for generations
Active at night, withdrawing before dawn
Helpful when respected; mischievous or destructive when mocked, watched, or formally rewarded

range.description: Hobgoblin and hob traditions are concentrated in England — particularly the north and Midlands — and in adjacent parts of the British Isles. Closely related figures appear in Scottish, Welsh, and Manx folklore.

first_recorded.source: 'Hob' attested as a name for a household sprite in English by c. 1530

peak_activity_periods.description: Robin Goodfellow chapbooks, Reformation-era polemics, and Shakespeare popularise the hobgoblin in English literary culture
Romantic and Victorian folklorists (e.g. Keightley, later Briggs) systematically catalogue British hob and brownie traditions

temporal.seasonal_patterns: Some traditions tie hobgoblin activity to the agricultural year, particularly threshing and dairying seasons.

origins.cultural_roots: Hobgoblin lore grows out of long-standing British and Anglo-Saxon traditions of household and farmstead spirits, fused with medieval European goblin and fairy traditions during and after the Christianisation of England.
origins.earliest_accounts: 'Hob' is attested in English as a name for a household sprite by c. 1530. The Robin Goodfellow / Puck strand is securely documented in Tudor and early Stuart literature.
origins.folklore_connections: Closely linked to brownies, boggarts, lobs, and the broader 'fairy' (faerie) tradition of the British Isles.

timeline.event: Earliest known English usage of 'hob' as a name for a household sprite
timeline.significance: Establishes the linguistic foundation of the hobgoblin tradition
timeline.source: Etymological surveys cited in the Wikipedia article on hobgoblin

timeline.event: William Shakespeare composes A Midsummer Night's Dream, identifying Puck with Robin Goodfellow and the hobgoblin
timeline.significance: The hobgoblin enters elite English literary culture and is fixed as a friendly trickster

timeline.event: Anonymous chapbook Robin Good-Fellow, His Mad Prankes and Merry Jests printed (circulating earlier in oral tradition)
timeline.significance: Popularises Robin Goodfellow as the canonical hobgoblin of English folklore
timeline.source: Robin Good-Fellow, His Mad Prankes and Merry Jests (1628)

history.modern_era: Hobgoblins remain a stock figure of English-speaking fantasy fiction, role-playing games, and children's literature. Modern fantasy gaming (most influentially Dungeons & Dragons from the 1970s onward) has further entrenched the inverted, malevolent sense of 'hobgoblin' as a larger, more militaristic goblin, pulling the term still further away from its original household-sprite meaning.

documentary.name: Robin Good-Fellow, His Mad Prankes and Merry Jests (1628)
documentary.description: Early printed chapbook collecting tales of Robin Goodfellow as a hobgoblin trickster of English folklore.
documentary.analysis: A literary record of folkloric tradition, not an evidentiary claim about a literal entity.
documentary.source: Robin Good-Fellow, His Mad Prankes and Merry Jests (1628)

paranormal.name: Domestic fairy / household spirit
paranormal.description: In traditional belief, the hobgoblin is one of a class of small fae or spirit beings attached to particular households, neither devil nor angel but a separate order of being subject to its own customs and taboos.
evidence_cited: Continuity of household-sprite traditions across the British Isles
Stable folk practices around offerings and taboos

skeptical.name: Folkloric personification of household labour
skeptical.description: Hobgoblin tales personify the unseen domestic and agricultural labour of pre-industrial households, providing supernatural framings for nocturnal sounds, missing objects, and unexplained tidying or mess.
evidence_cited: Embedment of hobgoblin behaviour in everyday domestic routine
Variation of hobgoblin lore with regional economic and household practices

stories: The hob of Hart Hall and similar northern English farmstead tales
Tales of hobgoblins driven away by gifts of new clothes
folklore.beliefs: Treated as a real category of household spirit in traditional belief, with codified rules of offerings, courteous conduct, and avoidance of formal payment.

literature: A Midsummer Night's Dream — William Shakespeare (c. 1595)
Robin Good-Fellow, His Mad Prankes and Merry Jests (1628)
An Encyclopedia of Fairies — Katharine Briggs (1976)
film_tv: Multiple film and stage adaptations of A Midsummer Night's Dream
Hobgoblins (1988, low-budget cult film, modern monstrous-goblin sense)
games: Dungeons & Dragons (1974–) — codifies the modern monstrous 'hobgoblin' as a larger goblinoid
Numerous fantasy video games featuring hobgoblins as a creature class
other: Hobgoblin Beer (Wychwood Brewery, UK) — a famous modern commercial use of the term

researchers.name: Thomas Keightley
researchers.contribution: The Fairy Mythology (1828); early systematic survey of British and European fairy lore including hobgoblins

researchers.name: Katharine Briggs
researchers.contribution: Comprehensive cataloguing of British fairy and hob traditions, including An Encyclopedia of Fairies (1976)

organizations.name: The Folklore Society
organizations.focus: Academic study of British and European folklore

research.ongoing_studies: Continuing academic study within British folklore and fairy-lore scholarship; the hobgoblin remains a standard topic in surveys of British fairy traditions.

sources.title: An Encyclopedia of Fairies: Hobgoblins, Brownies, Bogies, and Other Supernatural Creatures
